hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'Helvetiorum' — 'Helvetii' with -ii is nominative plural
hints.dative: Dative plural would be 'Helvetiis' — 'Helvetii' is nominative plural, the subject
hints.vocative: Caesar is describing the Helvetii, not addressing them

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive plural in -arum — 'Helvetiorum' with -orum is 2nd declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive plural in -um/-ium — 'Helvetiorum' is 2nd declension
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive plural in -uum — 'Helvetiorum' is 2nd declension

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'omnes' — 'omnium' with -ium is genitive plural
hints.accusative: Accusative plural would be 'omnes/omnia' — 'omnium' is genitive
hints.dative: Dative plural would be 'omnibus' — 'omnium' is genitive

hints.nominative: Nominative singular is 'res' — 'rerum' with -rum is genitive plural
hints.accusative: Accusative plural would be 'res' — 'rerum' is genitive plural
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'rebus' — 'rerum' is genitive plural

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'rei' with -ei is 5th declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'rei' with -ei is 5th declension
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'rei' with -ei is 5th declension

hints.nominative: While 'inopia' could be nominative, here it is ablative of cause with 'adducti'
hints.vocative: Vocative is not used here — 'inopia' is ablative expressing cause
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'inopiam' — 'inopia' here is ablative of cause

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'inopiae' with -ae is 1st declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'inopiae' with -ae is 1st declension
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'inopiae' with -ae is 1st declension

steps.reveal: adducti = having been driven/compelled (nom. masc. pl.)

hints.present active participle: Present active participle would be 'adducentes' — 'adducti' with -ti is perfect passive
hints.perfect active indicative: Perfect active would be 'adduxerunt' — 'adducti' is a participle, not finite verb
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'adducendi' — 'adducti' is perfect passive participle

hints.to add: That would be 'addere' — 'adducti' comes from 'adducere' (to lead to/compel)
hints.to lead away: That would be 'abducere' — 'adducere' means to lead TO, not away
hints.to produce: That would be 'producere' — 'adducere' means 'to lead to/compel'

hints.duco, ducere, duxi, ductum: These are the parts of 'ducere' (to lead) — 'adducere' is its compound ad + ducere
hints.abduco, abducere, abduxi, abductum: These are the parts of 'abducere' (to lead away) — different prefix
hints.addico, addicere, addixi, addictum: These are the parts of 'addicere' (to adjudge/assign)

translation: The Helvetii, compelled by a lack of all supplies
